questions.scenario: The deployment pipeline fails with `AuthorizationFailed` for `Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write` at subscription scope. The pipeline identity can deploy app resources in one resource group. What is the safest interpretation?

options.text: The pipeline can deploy some resources, but creating role assignments at subscription scope needs a different permission and should be reviewed carefully.
The pipeline is broken for all Azure operations, so replacing the service principal is the first fix.
The app resource group is unhealthy, so role assignments should be retried in a new group.
The region is not available, so Azure reports role assignment errors instead of service availability errors.

questions.explanation: The denied action is specifically role assignment creation at a broad scope. That is a sensitive permission. Existing deploy ability inside one group does not imply authority to grant access.

questions.scenario: The app fails after a secret rotation. Logs show it is still requesting version `v1`, while Key Vault has enabled version `v2` and disabled `v1`. Which fix direction matches the evidence?

options.text: Update the app configuration or secret reference so it no longer pins the disabled version, then verify the new version works.
Grant the app Owner on the subscription, because Owner can read disabled secret versions.
Move the vault into the app resource group, because version pinning is caused by group boundaries.
Add another managed identity, because multiple identities automatically choose the newest secret version.

questions.explanation: The problem is version selection and rotation behavior. Broader RBAC or another identity does not repair an app that still points at a disabled version.

questions.scenario: A developer says production secret reads work from their laptop using `az login`, but the deployed app gets `Forbidden`. What is the most likely confusion? The developer test used `az login` from a laptop, while production uses a managed identity attached to the running app revision.

options.text: The developer's user identity and the app's runtime identity are different callers with different permissions.
The laptop and the app share all Azure permissions once they use the same subscription.
The deployed app needs the developer to stay signed in for managed identity to work.
The production app cannot use Key Vault unless every developer also has vault access.

questions.explanation: This is a classic caller mix-up. A successful laptop test proves that the developer's identity can read the secret, not that the app's runtime identity can.

questions.scenario: Which fixes reduce risk when a production access issue appears? The incident is active, but the team knows every permission change will be reviewed afterward by the security owner.

options.text: Confirm the caller identity before granting new access.
Grant the smallest role at the smallest useful scope.
Record the reason, owner, and expiry or review date for unusual access.
Grant broad access first and plan to clean it up after the incident without recording why.

questions.explanation: Good incident fixes still preserve auditability and blast-radius control. Broad temporary access may happen in rare emergencies, but it should not be the default and should not be undocumented.

questions.scenario: An access review finds `Contributor` assigned to `All Engineers` at the production subscription. The team only needs a few people to restart the orders app and read logs. What is the best first remediation plan?

options.text: Replace the broad assignment with narrower roles and scopes that match restart and log-read tasks, then review who actually needs them.
Keep Contributor because production access is easier if everyone can fix anything.
Move production to a new region, because region separation is the right fix for broad RBAC.
Rename the group to `Production Operators`, because Azure treats clearer names as narrower permissions.

questions.explanation: The issue is excessive role and scope. Narrower roles, narrower scope, and group membership review reduce blast radius. Renaming or moving regions does not change the access power.
